items.correct: A drawing showing multiple 2D views (front, side, plan) of an object.
items.misconception: A 3D drawing showing the object at an angle.
items.random_wrong: A drawing in black ink only.
items.why: Students confuse orthographic (multi-view 2D) with isometric (single 3D view). Orthographic projects each face flat onto separate views - used for engineering specification.

items.q: What force does a rope in a tug-of-war experience?
items.correct: Tension - it is being pulled at both ends.
items.misconception: Compression - because it is squeezed by hands.
items.why: Students confuse the localised hand grip with the force through the rope. Pulling stretches the rope - that's tension. Compression would be pushing the ends together (which a rope cannot resist).

items.q: What determines whether a timber is a hardwood or softwood?
items.correct: The type of tree - deciduous (hardwood) or coniferous (softwood), not its hardness.
items.misconception: Whether the wood itself is physically hard or soft.
items.random_wrong: How long the tree took to grow.
items.why: Students assume 'hardwood' means hard. Balsa is a hardwood but very soft; yew is a softwood but harder than many hardwoods. Classification is BOTANICAL, not based on hardness.
It's Wood. “Metal” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Finer. “Coarser” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what fine sandpaper grit might you finish with?
items.why: The real figure is 240. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
